title: Science fiction film

infobox.value: (or **sci-fi**) is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that uses [speculative](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction"), [science](/wiki/Science "Science")-based depictions of phenomena that are not fully accepted by mainstream science, such as [extraterrestrial lifeforms](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life_in_fiction "Extraterrestrial life in fiction"), [spacecraft](/wiki/List_of_fictional_spacecraft "List of fictional spacecraft"), [robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot"), [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg"), [mutants](/wiki/Mutants_in_fiction "Mutants in fiction"), [interstellar travel](/wiki/Interstellar_travel "Interstellar travel"), [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel"), or other technologies. [Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") films have often been used to focus on [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") or [social issues](/wiki/Social_issue "Social issue"), and to explore philosophical issues like the [human condition](/wiki/Human_condition "Human condition").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Film genre

This article is about the genre specific to films. For the fiction genre in general, see [Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction"). For other uses, see [Science fiction (disambiguation)](/wiki/Science_fiction_(disambiguation) "Science fiction (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/97/Maschinenmensch_in_a_screen_shot_from_the_Metropolis_film.jpg/250px-Maschinenmensch_in_a_screen_shot_from_the_Metropolis_film.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maschinenmensch_in_a_screen_shot_from_the_Metropolis_film.jpg)

*[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* (1927) by [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang") was one of the first feature-length science fiction films. It was produced at [Studio Babelsberg](/wiki/Studio_Babelsberg "Studio Babelsberg"), Germany. (Image shows a scene from the film)

**Science fiction** (or **sci-fi**) is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") that uses [speculative](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction"), [science](/wiki/Science "Science")-based depictions of phenomena that are not fully accepted by mainstream science, such as [extraterrestrial lifeforms](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life_in_fiction "Extraterrestrial life in fiction"), [spacecraft](/wiki/List_of_fictional_spacecraft "List of fictional spacecraft"), [robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot"), [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg"), [mutants](/wiki/Mutants_in_fiction "Mutants in fiction"), [interstellar travel](/wiki/Interstellar_travel "Interstellar travel"), [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel"), or other technologies. [Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") films have often been used to focus on [political](/wiki/Politics "Politics") or [social issues](/wiki/Social_issue "Social issue"), and to explore philosophical issues like the [human condition](/wiki/Human_condition "Human condition").

The genre has existed since the early years of [silent cinema](/wiki/Silent_cinema "Silent cinema"), when [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès")' *[A Trip to the Moon](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")* (1902) employed [trick photography](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect") effects. The next major example (first in feature-length in the genre) was the film *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* (1927). There were a few early big-budget films, notably *[Things to Come](/wiki/Things_to_Come "Things to Come")* (1936), but from the 1930s to the 1950s the genre consisted mainly of low-budget [B movies](/wiki/B_movies "B movies"). After [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s landmark *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (1968), the science fiction film genre was taken more seriously. In the late 1970s, big-budget science fiction films filled with special effects became popular with audiences after the success of *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)")* (1977) and paved the way for the [blockbuster](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)") hits of subsequent decades.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Screenwriter and scholar [Eric R. Williams](/wiki/Eric_R._Williams "Eric R. Williams") identifies science fiction films as one of eleven super-genres in his [screenwriters’ taxonomy](/wiki/Screenwriters_Taxonomy "Screenwriters Taxonomy"), stating that all feature-length narrative films can be classified by these super-genres.  The other ten super-genres are [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film"), [crime](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film"), [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy_film "Fantasy film"), [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film"), [romance](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film"), [slice of life](/wiki/Slice_of_life "Slice of life"), [sports](/wiki/Sports_film "Sports film"), [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film"), [war](/wiki/War_film "War film"), and [western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Characteristics of the genre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics of the genre")]

According to [Vivian Sobchack](/wiki/Vivian_Sobchack "Vivian Sobchack"), a British cinema and media theorist and cultural critic:

> Science fiction film is a film genre which emphasizes actual, extrapolative, or 2.0 speculative [science](/wiki/Science "Science") and the [empirical method](/wiki/Empirical_methods "Empirical methods"), interacting in a social context with the lesser emphasized, but still present, [transcendentalism](/wiki/Transcendentalism "Transcendentalism") of [magic](/wiki/Magic_(paranormal) "Magic (paranormal)") and [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), in an attempt to reconcile man with the unknown.
>
> :   — Vivian Carol Sobchack, p. 63

This definition suggests a continuum between (real-world) [empiricism](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") and ([supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural")) [transcendentalism](/wiki/Transcendentalism "Transcendentalism"), with science fiction films on the side of empiricism, and happy films and sad films on the side of transcendentalism. However, there are numerous well-known examples of science fiction horror films, epitomized by such pictures as *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(1931) "Frankenstein (1931)")* and *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")*.

The visual style of science fiction film is characterized by a clash between alien and familiar images. This clash is implemented when alien images become familiar, as in *[A Clockwork Orange](/wiki/A_Clockwork_Orange_(film) "A Clockwork Orange (film)")*, when the repetitions of the Korova Milkbar make the alien decor seem more familiar.[[4]](#cite_note-4) As well, familiar images become alien, as in the films *[Repo Man](/wiki/Repo_Man_(film) "Repo Man (film)")* and *[Liquid Sky](/wiki/Liquid_Sky "Liquid Sky")*.[[5]](#cite_note-5) For example, in *[Dr. Strangelove](/wiki/Dr._Strangelove "Dr. Strangelove")*, the distortion of the humans make the familiar images seem more alien.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Finally, alien images are juxtaposed with the familiar, as in *[The Deadly Mantis](/wiki/The_Deadly_Mantis "The Deadly Mantis")*, when a giant [praying mantis](/wiki/Mantis "Mantis") is shown climbing the [Washington Monument](/wiki/Washington_Monument "Washington Monument").

Cultural theorist [Scott Bukatman](/wiki/Scott_Bukatman "Scott Bukatman") has proposed that science fiction film allows contemporary culture to witness an expression of the [sublime](/wiki/Sublime_(philosophy) "Sublime (philosophy)"), be it through exaggerated scale, apocalypse or transcendence.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of science fiction films](/wiki/History_of_science_fiction_films "History of science fiction films")

### 1900–1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1900–1920s")]

See also: [List of science fiction films before 1920](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_before_1920 "List of science fiction films before 1920") and [List of science fiction films of the 1920s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1920s "List of science fiction films of the 1920s")

*[L'uomo meccanico](/wiki/The_Mechanical_Man "The Mechanical Man")* (1921)

Science fiction films appeared early in the [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") era, typically as short films shot in black and white, sometimes with colour tinting. They usually had a technological theme and were often intended to be humorous. In [1902](/wiki/1902_in_film "1902 in film"), [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès") released *[Le Voyage dans la Lune](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")*, generally considered the first science fiction film,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and a film that used early trick photography to depict a spacecraft's journey to the Moon. Several early films merged the science fiction and [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") genres. Examples of this are *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(1910_film) "Frankenstein (1910 film)")* ([1910](/wiki/1910_in_film "1910 in film")), a film adaptation of [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley")'s novel, and *[Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde](/wiki/Dr._Jekyll_and_Mr._Hyde_(1920_Paramount_film) "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde (1920 Paramount film)")* (1920), based on the psychological tale by [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"). Taking a more adventurous tack, *[20,000 Leagues Under the Sea](/wiki/20,000_Leagues_Under_the_Sea_(1916_film) "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea (1916 film)")* ([1916](/wiki/1916_in_film "1916 in film")) is a film based on [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne")’s famous novel of a wondrous submarine and its vengeful captain. In the 1920s, European filmmakers tended to use science fiction for prediction and social commentary, as can be seen in German films such as *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* ([1927](/wiki/1927_in_film "1927 in film")) and *[Frau im Mond](/wiki/Frau_im_Mond "Frau im Mond")* ([1929](/wiki/1929_in_film "1929 in film")). Other notable science fiction films of the silent era include *[The Impossible Voyage](/wiki/The_Impossible_Voyage "The Impossible Voyage")* (1904), *The Motorist* (1906), *[The Conquest of the Pole](/wiki/The_Conquest_of_the_Pole "The Conquest of the Pole")* (1912), *[Himmelskibet](/wiki/Himmelskibet_(film) "Himmelskibet (film)")* (1918; which with its runtime of 97 minutes generally is considered the first feature-length science fiction film in history),[[8]](#cite_note-8) *[The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari](/wiki/The_Cabinet_of_Dr._Caligari "The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari")* (1920), *[The Mechanical Man](/wiki/The_Mechanical_Man "The Mechanical Man")* (1921), *[Paris Qui Dort](/wiki/Paris_Qui_Dort "Paris Qui Dort")* (1923), *[Aelita](/wiki/Aelita_(film) "Aelita (film)")* (1924), *[Luch Smerti](/wiki/Luch_Smerti "Luch Smerti")* (1925), and *[The Lost World](/wiki/The_Lost_World_(1925_film) "The Lost World (1925 film)")* (1925).

### 1930s–1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1930s–1950s")]

See also: [List of science fiction films of the 1930s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1930s "List of science fiction films of the 1930s"), [List of science fiction films of the 1940s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1940s "List of science fiction films of the 1940s"), and [List of science fiction films of the 1950s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1950s "List of science fiction films of the 1950s")

In the 1930s, there were several big budget science fiction films, notably *[Just Imagine](/wiki/Just_Imagine_(film) "Just Imagine (film)")* (1930), *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(1933_film) "King Kong (1933 film)")* (1933) and *[Things to Come](/wiki/Things_to_Come "Things to Come")* (1936). Starting in 1936, a number of science fiction [comic strips](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip") were adapted as [serials](/wiki/Serial_film "Serial film"), notably *[Flash Gordon](/wiki/Flash_Gordon_(serial) "Flash Gordon (serial)")* and *[Buck Rogers](/wiki/Buck_Rogers_(serial) "Buck Rogers (serial)")*, both starring [Buster Crabbe](/wiki/Buster_Crabbe "Buster Crabbe"). These serials, and the comic strips they were based on, were very popular with the general public. Other notable science fiction films of the 1930s include *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(1931_film) "Frankenstein (1931 film)")* (1931), *[Bride of Frankenstein](/wiki/Bride_of_Frankenstein "Bride of Frankenstein")* (1935), *[Doctor X](/wiki/Doctor_X_(film) "Doctor X (film)")* (1932), *[Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde](/wiki/Dr._Jekyll_and_Mr._Hyde_(1931_film) "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde (1931 film)")* (1931), *[F.P.1](/wiki/F.P.1 "F.P.1")* (1932), *[Island of Lost Souls](/wiki/Island_of_Lost_Souls_(1932_film) "Island of Lost Souls (1932 film)")* (1932), *[Deluge](/wiki/Deluge_(film) "Deluge (film)")* (1933), *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man_(1933_film) "The Invisible Man (1933 film)")* (1933), *[Master of the World](/wiki/Master_of_the_World_(1934_film) "Master of the World (1934 film)")* (1934), *[Mad Love](/wiki/Mad_Love_(1935_film) "Mad Love (1935 film)")* (1935), *[Trans-Atlantic Tunnel](/wiki/The_Tunnel_(1935_film) "The Tunnel (1935 film)")* (1935), *[The Devil-Doll](/wiki/The_Devil-Doll "The Devil-Doll")* (1936), *[The Invisible Ray](/wiki/The_Invisible_Ray_(1936_film) "The Invisible Ray (1936 film)")* (1936), *[The Man Who Changed His Mind](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Changed_His_Mind "The Man Who Changed His Mind")* (1936), *[The Walking Dead](/wiki/The_Walking_Dead_(1936_film) "The Walking Dead (1936 film)")* (1936), *[Non-Stop New York](/wiki/Non-Stop_New_York "Non-Stop New York")* (1937), and *[The Return of Doctor X](/wiki/The_Return_of_Doctor_X "The Return of Doctor X")* (1939). The 1940s brought us *[Before I Hang](/wiki/Before_I_Hang "Before I Hang")* (1940), *[Black Friday](/wiki/Black_Friday_(1940_film) "Black Friday (1940 film)")* (1940), *[Dr. Cyclops](/wiki/Dr._Cyclops "Dr. Cyclops")* (1940), *[The Devil Commands](/wiki/The_Devil_Commands "The Devil Commands")* (1941), *[Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde](/wiki/Dr._Jekyll_and_Mr._Hyde_(1941_film) "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde (1941 film)")* (1941), *[Man Made Monster](/wiki/Man_Made_Monster "Man Made Monster")* (1941), *[It Happened Tomorrow](/wiki/It_Happened_Tomorrow "It Happened Tomorrow")* (1944), *[It Happens Every Spring](/wiki/It_Happens_Every_Spring "It Happens Every Spring")* (1949), and *[The Perfect Woman](/wiki/The_Perfect_Woman_(1949_film) "The Perfect Woman (1949 film)")* (1949). The release of *[Destination Moon](/wiki/Destination_Moon_(film) "Destination Moon (film)")* (1950) and *[Rocketship X-M](/wiki/Rocketship_X-M "Rocketship X-M")* (1950) brought us to what many people consider "the golden age of the science fiction film".

In the 1950s, public interest in space travel and new technologies was great. While many 1950s science fiction films were low-budget [B movies](/wiki/B_movie "B movie"), there were several successful films with larger budgets and impressive special effects. These include *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still "The Day the Earth Stood Still")* (1951), *[The Thing from Another World](/wiki/The_Thing_from_Another_World "The Thing from Another World")* (1951), *[When Worlds Collide](/wiki/When_Worlds_Collide_(1951_film) "When Worlds Collide (1951 film)")* (1951), *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds_(1953_film) "The War of the Worlds (1953 film)")* (1953), *[20,000 Leagues Under the Sea](/wiki/20,000_Leagues_Under_the_Sea_(1954_film) "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea (1954 film)")* (1954), *[This Island Earth](/wiki/This_Island_Earth "This Island Earth")* (1955), *[Forbidden Planet](/wiki/Forbidden_Planet "Forbidden Planet")* (1956), *[Invasion of the Body Snatchers](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Body_Snatchers "Invasion of the Body Snatchers")* (1956), *[The Curse of Frankenstein](/wiki/The_Curse_of_Frankenstein "The Curse of Frankenstein")* (1957), *[Journey to the Center of the Earth](/wiki/Journey_to_the_Center_of_the_Earth_(1959_film) "Journey to the Center of the Earth (1959 film)")* (1959) and *[On the Beach](/wiki/On_the_Beach_(1959_film) "On the Beach (1959 film)")* (1959). There is often a close connection between films in the science fiction genre and the so-called "[monster movie](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie")". Examples of this are *[Them!](/wiki/Them! "Them!")* (1954), *[The Beast from 20,000 Fathoms](/wiki/The_Beast_from_20,000_Fathoms "The Beast from 20,000 Fathoms")* (1953) and *[The Blob](/wiki/The_Blob "The Blob")* (1958). During the 1950s, [Ray Harryhausen](/wiki/Ray_Harryhausen "Ray Harryhausen"), protege of master King Kong animator Willis O'Brien, used [stop-motion animation](/wiki/Stop_motion "Stop motion") to create special effects for the following notable science fiction films: *[It Came from Beneath the Sea](/wiki/It_Came_from_Beneath_the_Sea "It Came from Beneath the Sea")* (1955), *[Earth vs. the Flying Saucers](/wiki/Earth_vs._the_Flying_Saucers "Earth vs. the Flying Saucers")* (1956) and *[20 Million Miles to Earth](/wiki/20_Million_Miles_to_Earth "20 Million Miles to Earth")* (1957).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Gojira_1954_Japanese_poster.jpg/250px-Gojira_1954_Japanese_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gojira_1954_Japanese_poster.jpg)

Poster for the 1954 Japanese monster film *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1954_film) "Godzilla (1954 film)")*

The most successful monster movies were Japanese film studio [Toho](/wiki/Toho "Toho")'s *[kaiju](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju")* films directed by [Ishirō Honda](/wiki/Ishir%C5%8D_Honda "Ishirō Honda") and featuring special effects by [Eiji Tsuburaya](/wiki/Eiji_Tsuburaya "Eiji Tsuburaya").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The 1954 film *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1954_film) "Godzilla (1954 film)")*, with the title monster attacking Tokyo, gained immense popularity, spawned multiple sequels, led to other kaiju films like *[Rodan](/wiki/Rodan_(film) "Rodan (film)")*, and created one of the most recognizable monsters in cinema history. [Japanese science fiction](/wiki/Japanese_science_fiction "Japanese science fiction") films, particularly the [tokusatsu](/wiki/Tokusatsu "Tokusatsu") and kaiju genres, were known for their extensive use of [special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect"), and gained worldwide popularity in the 1950s. Kaiju and tokusatsu films, notably *[Warning from Space](/wiki/Warning_from_Space "Warning from Space")* (1956), sparked [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s interest in science fiction films and influenced *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (1968). According to his biographer [John Baxter](/wiki/John_Baxter_(author) "John Baxter (author)"), despite their "clumsy model sequences, the films were often well-photographed in colour ... and their dismal dialogue was delivered in well-designed and well-lit sets."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### 1960s–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1960s–present")]

See also: [List of science fiction films of the 1960s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1960s "List of science fiction films of the 1960s")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Discovery_One_from_trailer_of_2001_A_Space_Odyssey_%281968%29.png/250px-Discovery_One_from_trailer_of_2001_A_Space_Odyssey_%281968%29.png)](/wiki/File:Discovery_One_from_trailer_of_2001_A_Space_Odyssey_(1968).png)

*[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey "2001: A Space Odyssey")*, the landmark 1968 collaboration between filmmaker [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") and classic science-fiction author [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke"), featured groundbreaking special effects, such as the realization of the spaceship USSC *[Discovery One](/wiki/Discovery_One "Discovery One")* (pictured here).

With the [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") between the USSR and the US going on, documentaries and illustrations of actual events, pioneers and technology were plenty. Any movie featuring realistic space travel was at risk of being obsolete at its time of release, rather fossil than fiction. There were relatively few science fiction films in the 1960s, but some of the films transformed science fiction cinema. [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* ([1968](/wiki/1968_in_film "1968 in film")) brought new realism to the genre, with its groundbreaking visual effects and realistic portrayal of space travel and influenced the genre with its epic story and transcendent philosophical scope. Other 1960s films included *[Planet of the Vampires](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Vampires "Planet of the Vampires")* (1965) by Italian filmmaker [Mario Bava](/wiki/Mario_Bava "Mario Bava"), that is regarded as one of the best movies of the period, *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(1968_film) "Planet of the Apes (1968 film)")* (1968) and *[Fahrenheit 451](/wiki/Fahrenheit_451_(1966_film) "Fahrenheit 451 (1966 film)")* ([1966](/wiki/1966_in_film "1966 in film")), which provided social commentary, and the campy *[Barbarella](/wiki/Barbarella_(film) "Barbarella (film)")* (1968), which explored the comical side of earlier science fiction. [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard")'s French "new wave" film *[Alphaville](/wiki/Alphaville_(film) "Alphaville (film)")* (1965) posited a futuristic Paris commanded by an artificial intelligence which has outlawed all emotion.

See also: [List of science fiction films of the 1970s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1970s "List of science fiction films of the 1970s") and [List of science fiction films of the 1980s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1980s "List of science fiction films of the 1980s")

The era of crewed trips to the Moon in 1969 and the 1970s saw a resurgence of interest in the science fiction film. [Andrei Tarkovsky](/wiki/Andrei_Tarkovsky "Andrei Tarkovsky")'s *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(1972_film) "Solaris (1972 film)")* ([1972](/wiki/1972_in_film "1972 in film")) and *[Stalker](/wiki/Stalker_(1979_film) "Stalker (1979 film)")* ([1979](/wiki/1979_in_film "1979 in film")) are two widely acclaimed examples of the renewed interest of [film *auteurs*](/wiki/Auteur_theory "Auteur theory") in science fiction.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Science fiction films from the early 1970s explored the theme of paranoia, in which humanity is depicted as under threat from sociological, ecological or technological adversaries of its own creation, such as [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas")'s directional debut *[THX 1138](/wiki/THX_1138 "THX 1138")* ([1971](/wiki/1971_in_film "1971 in film")), *[The Andromeda Strain](/wiki/The_Andromeda_Strain_(film) "The Andromeda Strain (film)")* ([1971](/wiki/1971_in_film "1971 in film")), *[Silent Running](/wiki/Silent_Running "Silent Running")* ([1972](/wiki/1972_in_film "1972 in film")), *[Soylent Green](/wiki/Soylent_Green "Soylent Green")* ([1973](/wiki/1973_in_film "1973 in film")), *[Westworld](/wiki/Westworld_(film) "Westworld (film)")* ([1973](/wiki/1973_in_film "1973 in film")) and its sequel *[Futureworld](/wiki/Futureworld "Futureworld")* ([1976](/wiki/1976_in_film "1976 in film")), and *[Logan's Run](/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_(film) "Logan's Run (film)")* ([1976](/wiki/1976_in_film "1976 in film")). The science fiction comedies of the 1970s included [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen")'s *[Sleeper](/wiki/Sleeper_(1973_film) "Sleeper (1973 film)")* ([1973](/wiki/1973_in_film "1973 in film")), and [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter")'s *[Dark Star](/wiki/Dark_Star_(film) "Dark Star (film)")* ([1974](/wiki/1974_in_film "1974 in film")). The sports science fiction genre can be seen in films such as [Rollerball](/wiki/Rollerball_(1975_film) "Rollerball (1975 film)") (1975).

*[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)")* ([1977](/wiki/1977_in_film "1977 in film")) and *[Close Encounters of the Third Kind](/wiki/Close_Encounters_of_the_Third_Kind "Close Encounters of the Third Kind")* ([1977](/wiki/1977_in_film "1977 in film")) were box-office hits that brought about a huge increase in science fiction films. In [1979](/wiki/1979_in_film "1979 in film"), *[Star Trek: The Motion Picture](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Motion_Picture "Star Trek: The Motion Picture")* brought the television series to the big screen for the first time. It was also in this period that the Walt Disney Company released many science fiction films for family audiences such as *[The Black Hole](/wiki/The_Black_Hole_(1979_film) "The Black Hole (1979 film)")*, *[Flight of the Navigator](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Navigator "Flight of the Navigator")*, and *[Honey, I Shrunk the Kids](/wiki/Honey,_I_Shrunk_the_Kids "Honey, I Shrunk the Kids")*. The sequels to *Star Wars*, *[The Empire Strikes Back](/wiki/The_Empire_Strikes_Back "The Empire Strikes Back")* ([1980](/wiki/1980_in_film "1980 in film")) and *[Return of the Jedi](/wiki/Return_of_the_Jedi "Return of the Jedi")* ([1983](/wiki/1983_in_film "1983 in film")), also saw worldwide box office success. [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott")'s films, such as *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")* ([1979](/wiki/1979_in_film "1979 in film")) and *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* ([1982](/wiki/1982_in_film "1982 in film")), along with [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron")'s *[The Terminator](/wiki/The_Terminator "The Terminator")* ([1984](/wiki/1984_in_film "1984 in film")), presented the future as dark, dirty and chaotic, and depicted aliens and androids as hostile and dangerous. In contrast, [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial](/wiki/E.T._the_Extra-Terrestrial "E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial")* ([1982](/wiki/1982_in_film "1982 in film")), one of the most successful films of the 1980s, presented aliens as benign and friendly, a theme already present in Spielberg's own *Close Encounters of the Third Kind*. James Bond also entered the science fiction genre in 1979 with *[Moonraker](/wiki/Moonraker_(film) "Moonraker (film)")*.

The big budget adaptations of [Frank Herbert](/wiki/Frank_Herbert "Frank Herbert")'s *[Dune](/wiki/Dune_(1984_film) "Dune (1984 film)")* and [Alex Raymond](/wiki/Alex_Raymond "Alex Raymond")'s *[Flash Gordon](/wiki/Flash_Gordon_(film) "Flash Gordon (film)")*, as well as [Peter Hyams](/wiki/Peter_Hyams "Peter Hyams")'s sequel to *2001*, *[2010: The Year We Make Contact](/wiki/2010:_The_Year_We_Make_Contact "2010: The Year We Make Contact")* (based on *2001* author [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke")'s sequel novel *[2010: Odyssey Two](/wiki/2010:_Odyssey_Two "2010: Odyssey Two")*), were box office failures that dissuaded producers from investing in science fiction literary properties. Disney's *[Tron](/wiki/Tron "Tron")* ([1982](/wiki/1982_in_film "1982 in film")) turned out to be a moderate success. The strongest contributors to the genre during the second half of the 1980s were James Cameron and Paul Verhoeven with *[The Terminator](/wiki/The_Terminator "The Terminator")* and *[RoboCop](/wiki/RoboCop "RoboCop")* entries. [Robert Zemeckis](/wiki/Robert_Zemeckis "Robert Zemeckis")' film *[Back to the Future](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future "Back to the Future")* ([1985](/wiki/1985_in_film "1985 in film")) and its [sequels](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future_(franchise) "Back to the Future (franchise)") were critically praised and became box office successes, not to mention international phenomena. James Cameron's sequel to *Alien*, *[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(film) "Aliens (film)")* ([1986](/wiki/1986_in_film "1986 in film")), was very different from the original film, falling more into the action/science fiction genre, it was both a critical and commercial success and [Sigourney Weaver](/wiki/Sigourney_Weaver "Sigourney Weaver") was nominated for [Best Actress in a Leading Role](/wiki/Best_Actress_in_a_Leading_Role "Best Actress in a Leading Role") at the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"). The [Japanese cyberpunk](/wiki/Japanese_cyberpunk "Japanese cyberpunk") [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") film *[Akira](/wiki/Akira_(1988_film) "Akira (1988 film)")* ([1988](/wiki/1988_in_film "1988 in film")) also had a big influence outside Japan when released.

See also: [List of science fiction films of the 1990s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_1990s "List of science fiction films of the 1990s") and [List of science fiction films of the 2000s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_2000s "List of science fiction films of the 2000s")

In the 1990s, the emergence of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web") and the [cyberpunk](/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") genre spawned several movies on the theme of the computer-human interface, such as *[Terminator 2: Judgment Day](/wiki/Terminator_2:_Judgment_Day "Terminator 2: Judgment Day")* ([1991](/wiki/1991_in_film "1991 in film")), *[Total Recall](/wiki/Total_Recall_(1990_film) "Total Recall (1990 film)")* ([1990](/wiki/1990_in_film "1990 in film")), *[The Lawnmower Man](/wiki/The_Lawnmower_Man_(film) "The Lawnmower Man (film)")* ([1992](/wiki/1992_in_film "1992 in film")), and *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")* ([1999](/wiki/1999_in_film "1999 in film")). Other themes included disaster films (e.g., *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")* and *[Deep Impact](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(film) "Deep Impact (film)")*, both [1998](/wiki/1998_in_film "1998 in film")), alien invasion (e.g., *[Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(1996_film) "Independence Day (1996 film)")* ([1996](/wiki/1996_in_film "1996 in film"))) and genetic experimentation (e.g., *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(film) "Jurassic Park (film)")* ([1993](/wiki/1993_in_film "1993 in film")) and *[Gattaca](/wiki/Gattaca "Gattaca")* ([1997](/wiki/1997_in_film "1997 in film"))). Also, the *Star Wars* prequel trilogy began with the release of *[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")*, which eventually grossed over one billion dollars.

As the decade progressed, computers played an increasingly important role in both the addition of [special effects](/wiki/Special_effects "Special effects") (thanks to *[Terminator 2: Judgment Day](/wiki/Terminator_2:_Judgment_Day "Terminator 2: Judgment Day")* and *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(film) "Jurassic Park (film)")*) and the production of films. As software developed in sophistication it was used to produce more complicated effects. It also enabled filmmakers to enhance the visual quality of animation, resulting in films such as *[Ghost in the Shell](/wiki/Ghost_in_the_Shell_(1995_film) "Ghost in the Shell (1995 film)")* (1995) from Japan, and *[The Iron Giant](/wiki/The_Iron_Giant "The Iron Giant")* (1999) from the United States.

During the first decade of the 2000s, [superhero films](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") abounded, as did earthbound science fiction such as the *Matrix* trilogy. In [2005](/wiki/2005_in_film "2005 in film"), the *Star Wars* saga was completed (although it was later continued, but at the time it was not intended to be) with the darkly themed *[Star Wars: Episode III – Revenge of the Sith](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_III_%E2%80%93_Revenge_of_the_Sith "Star Wars: Episode III – Revenge of the Sith")*. Science-fiction also returned as a tool for political commentary in films such as *[A.I. Artificial Intelligence](/wiki/A.I._Artificial_Intelligence "A.I. Artificial Intelligence")*, *[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")*, *[Sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine_(2007_film) "Sunshine (2007 film)")*, *[District 9](/wiki/District_9 "District 9")*, *[Children of Men](/wiki/Children_of_Men "Children of Men")*, *[Serenity](/wiki/Serenity_(2005_film) "Serenity (2005 film)")*, *[Sleep Dealer](/wiki/Sleep_Dealer "Sleep Dealer")*, and *[Pandorum](/wiki/Pandorum "Pandorum")*. The 2000s also saw the release of *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film) "Transformers (film)")* (2007) and *[Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen](/wiki/Transformers:_Revenge_of_the_Fallen "Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen")* (2009), both of which resulted in worldwide box office success. In 2009, [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron")'s *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")* garnered worldwide box office success, and would later become the highest-grossing movie of all time. This movie was also an example of political commentary. It depicted humans destroying the environment on another planet by mining for a special metal called unobtainium. That same year, *[Terminator Salvation](/wiki/Terminator_Salvation "Terminator Salvation")* was released and garnered only moderate success.

Further information: [List of science fiction films of the 2010s](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_films_of_the_2010s "List of science fiction films of the 2010s")

The 2010s saw new entries in several classic science fiction franchises, including *[Predators](/wiki/Predators_(2010_film) "Predators (2010 film)")* ([2010](/wiki/2010_in_film "2010 in film")), *[Tron: Legacy](/wiki/Tron:_Legacy "Tron: Legacy")* (2010), a resurgence of the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* series, and entries into the *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes "Planet of the Apes")* and *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla "Godzilla")* franchises. Several more cross-genre films have also been produced, including comedies such as *[Hot Tub Time Machine](/wiki/Hot_Tub_Time_Machine "Hot Tub Time Machine")* (2010), *[Seeking a Friend for the End of the World](/wiki/Seeking_a_Friend_for_the_End_of_the_World "Seeking a Friend for the End of the World")* ([2012](/wiki/2012_in_film "2012 in film")), *[Safety Not Guaranteed](/wiki/Safety_Not_Guaranteed "Safety Not Guaranteed")* ([2013](/wiki/2013_in_film "2013 in film")), and *[Pixels](/wiki/Pixels_(2015_film) "Pixels (2015 film)")* (2015), romance films such as *[Her](/wiki/Her_(2013_film) "Her (2013 film)")* (2013), *[Monsters](/wiki/Monsters_(2010_film) "Monsters (2010 film)")* (2010), and *[Ex Machina](/wiki/Ex_Machina_(film) "Ex Machina (film)")* (2015), heist films including *[Inception](/wiki/Inception "Inception")* (2010) and action films including *[Real Steel](/wiki/Real_Steel "Real Steel")* (2011), *[Total Recall](/wiki/Total_Recall_(2012_film) "Total Recall (2012 film)")* (2012), *[Edge of Tomorrow](/wiki/Edge_of_Tomorrow "Edge of Tomorrow")* ([2014](/wiki/2014_in_film "2014 in film")), *[Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_(film) "Pacific Rim (film)")* (2013), *[Chappie](/wiki/Chappie_(film) "Chappie (film)")* (2015), *[Tomorrowland](/wiki/Tomorrowland_(film) "Tomorrowland (film)")* (2015), and *[Ghost in the Shell](/wiki/Ghost_in_the_Shell_(2017_film) "Ghost in the Shell (2017 film)")* (2017). The [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") boom has also continued, into films such as *[Iron Man 2](/wiki/Iron_Man_2 "Iron Man 2")* (2010) and *[Iron Man 3](/wiki/Iron_Man_3 "Iron Man 3")* (2013), several entries into the [X-Men film series](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)"), and *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* (2012), which became the fourth-highest-grossing film of all time. New franchises such as *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")* and *[Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film) "Guardians of the Galaxy (film)")* also began in this decade.

Further into the decade, more realistic science fiction [epic films](/wiki/Epic_film "Epic film") also become prevalent, including *[Battleship](/wiki/Battleship_(film) "Battleship (film)")* (2012), *[Gravity](/wiki/Gravity_(2013_film) "Gravity (2013 film)")* (2013), *[Elysium](/wiki/Elysium_(film) "Elysium (film)")* (2013), *[Interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_(film) "Interstellar (film)")* ([2014](/wiki/2014_in_film "2014 in film")), *[Mad Max: Fury Road](/wiki/Mad_Max:_Fury_Road "Mad Max: Fury Road")* ([2015](/wiki/2015_in_film "2015 in film")), *[The Martian](/wiki/The_Martian_(film) "The Martian (film)")* ([2015](/wiki/2015_in_film "2015 in film")), *[Arrival](/wiki/Arrival_(film) "Arrival (film)")* ([2016](/wiki/2016_in_film "2016 in film")), *[Passengers](/wiki/Passengers_(2016_film) "Passengers (2016 film)")* (2016), and *[Blade Runner 2049](/wiki/Blade_Runner_2049 "Blade Runner 2049")* ([2017](/wiki/2017_in_film "2017 in film")). Many of these films have gained widespread accolades, including several [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") wins and nominations. These films have addressed recent matters of scientific interest, including space travel, climate change, and artificial intelligence.

Alongside these original films, many adaptations were produced, especially within the [young adult](/wiki/Young_adult_fiction "Young adult fiction") [dystopian fiction](/wiki/Dystopian_fiction "Dystopian fiction") subgenre, popular in the early part of the decade. These include the [*Hunger Games* film series](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games_(film_series) "The Hunger Games (film series)"), based on the [trilogy of novels](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games "The Hunger Games") by [Suzanne Collins](/wiki/Suzanne_Collins "Suzanne Collins"), *[The Divergent Series](/wiki/The_Divergent_Series "The Divergent Series")* based on [Veronica Roth](/wiki/Veronica_Roth "Veronica Roth")'s [Divergent trilogy](/wiki/Divergent_trilogy "Divergent trilogy"), and the [*Maze Runner* series](/wiki/Maze_Runner_(film_series) "Maze Runner (film series)"), based on [James Dashner](/wiki/James_Dashner "James Dashner")'s [*The Maze Runner* novels](/wiki/The_Maze_Runner_(novel_series) "The Maze Runner (novel series)"). Several adult adaptations have also been produced, including *[The Martian](/wiki/The_Martian_(film) "The Martian (film)")* (2015), based on [Andy Weir](/wiki/Andy_Weir "Andy Weir")'s [2011 novel](/wiki/The_Martian_(Weir_novel) "The Martian (Weir novel)"), *[Cloud Atlas](/wiki/Cloud_Atlas_(film) "Cloud Atlas (film)")* (2012), based on [David Mitchell](/wiki/David_Mitchell_(author) "David Mitchell (author)")'s [2004 novel](/wiki/Cloud_Atlas_(novel) "Cloud Atlas (novel)"), *[World War Z](/wiki/World_War_Z_(film) "World War Z (film)")*, based on [Max Brooks](/wiki/Max_Brooks "Max Brooks")' [2006 novel](/wiki/World_War_Z "World War Z"), and *[Ready Player One](/wiki/Ready_Player_One_(film) "Ready Player One (film)")* (2018), based on [Ernest Cline](/wiki/Ernest_Cline "Ernest Cline")'s [2011 novel](/wiki/Ready_Player_One "Ready Player One").

Independent productions also increased in the 2010s, with the rise of [digital filmmaking](/wiki/Digital_cinematography "Digital cinematography") making it easier for filmmakers to produce movies on a smaller budget. These films include *[Attack the Block](/wiki/Attack_the_Block "Attack the Block")* (2011), *[Source Code](/wiki/Source_Code_(film) "Source Code (film)")* (2011), *[Looper](/wiki/Looper_(film) "Looper (film)")* (2012), *[Upstream Color](/wiki/Upstream_Color "Upstream Color")* (2013), *[Ex Machina](/wiki/Ex_Machina_(film) "Ex Machina (film)")* (2015), and *[Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets](/wiki/Valerian_and_the_City_of_a_Thousand_Planets "Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets")* (2017). In 2016, *Ex Machina* won the Academy Award for [Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") in a surprising upset over the much higher-budget *[Star Wars: The Force Awakens](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Force_Awakens "Star Wars: The Force Awakens")* (2015).

## Themes, imagery, and visual elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Themes, imagery, and visual elements")]

Science fiction films are often speculative in nature, and often include key supporting elements of science and technology. However, as often as not the "science" in a [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") science fiction movie can be considered pseudo-science, relying primarily on atmosphere and quasi-scientific artistic fancy than facts and conventional scientific theory. The definition can also vary depending on the viewpoint of the observer.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many science fiction films include elements of mysticism, [occult](/wiki/Occult "Occult"), magic, or the [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural"), considered by some to be more properly elements of fantasy or the occult (or religious) film.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This transforms the movie genre into a science fantasy with a religious or quasi-religious philosophy serving as the driving motivation. The movie *[Forbidden Planet](/wiki/Forbidden_Planet "Forbidden Planet")* employs many common science fiction elements, but the film carries a profound message - that the evolution of a species toward technological perfection (in this case exemplified by the disappeared alien civilization called the "Krell") does not ensure the loss of primitive and dangerous urges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the film, this part of the primitive mind manifests itself as monstrous destructive force emanating from the Freudian subconscious, or "Id".

Some films blur the line between the genres, such as films where the protagonist gains the extraordinary powers of the [superhero](/wiki/Superhero "Superhero"). These films usually employ quasi-plausible reason for the hero gaining these powers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Not all [science fiction themes](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_themes "List of science fiction themes") are equally suitable for movies. Science fiction horror is most common. Often enough, these films could just as well pass as [Westerns](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") or [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") films if the science fiction props were removed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Common motifs also include voyages and expeditions to other planets, and [dystopias](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia"), while [utopias](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") are rare.

### Imagery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Imagery")]

Film theorist [Vivian Sobchack](/wiki/Vivian_Sobchack "Vivian Sobchack") argues that science fiction films differ from fantasy films in that while science fiction film seeks to achieve our belief in the images we are viewing, fantasy film instead attempts to suspend our disbelief. The science fiction film displays the unfamiliar and alien in the context of the familiar. Despite the alien nature of the scenes and science fictional elements of the setting, the imagery of the film is related back to humankind and how we relate to our surroundings. While the science fiction film strives to push the boundaries of the human experience, they remain bound to the conditions and understanding of the audience and thereby contain prosaic aspects, rather than being completely alien or abstract.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Genre films such as westerns or war movies are bound to a particular area or time period. This is not true of the science fiction film. However, there are several common visual elements that are evocative of the genre. These include the spacecraft or space station, alien worlds or creatures, robots, and futuristic gadgets. Examples include movies like *[Lost in Space](/wiki/Lost_in_Space_(film) "Lost in Space (film)")*, *[Serenity](/wiki/Serenity_(2005_film) "Serenity (2005 film)")*, *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, *[Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus_(2012_film) "Prometheus (2012 film)")*, *[Tomorrowland](/wiki/Tomorrowland_(film) "Tomorrowland (film)")*, *[Passengers](/wiki/Passengers_(2016_film) "Passengers (2016 film)")*, and *[Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets](/wiki/Valerian_and_the_City_of_a_Thousand_Planets "Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets")*. More subtle visual clues can appear with changes of the human form through modifications in appearance, size, or behavior, or by means a known environment turned eerily alien, such as an empty city *[The Omega Man](/wiki/The_Omega_Man "The Omega Man")* (1971).

### Scientific elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Scientific elements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Dr._Strangelove.png/250px-Dr._Strangelove.png)](/wiki/File:Dr._Strangelove.png)

[Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers") as the titular character from *[Dr. Strangelove](/wiki/Dr._Strangelove "Dr. Strangelove")* (1964)

While science is a major element of this genre, many movie studios take significant liberties with scientific knowledge. Such liberties can be most readily observed in films that show [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") maneuvering in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"). The [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") should preclude the transmission of [sound](/wiki/Sound "Sound") or maneuvers employing wings, yet the soundtrack is filled with inappropriate flying noises and changes in flight path resembling an aircraft banking. The filmmakers, unfamiliar with the specifics of [space travel](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight"), focus instead on providing acoustical atmosphere and the more familiar maneuvers of the aircraft.

Similar instances of ignoring science in favor of art can be seen when movies present environmental effects as portrayed in *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* and *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(film_series) "Star Trek (film series)")*. Entire [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet") are destroyed in titanic explosions requiring mere seconds, whereas an actual event of this nature takes many hours.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The role of the scientist has varied considerably in the science fiction film genre, depending on the public perception of science and advanced technology.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Starting with [Dr. Frankenstein](/wiki/Victor_Frankenstein "Victor Frankenstein"), the [mad scientist](/wiki/Mad_scientist "Mad scientist") became a [stock character](/wiki/Stock_character "Stock character") who posed a dire threat to society and perhaps even civilization. Certain portrayals of the "mad scientist", such as [Peter Sellers](/wiki/Peter_Sellers "Peter Sellers")'s performance in *Dr. Strangelove*, have become iconic to the genre.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the monster films of the 1950s, the scientist often played a heroic role as the only person who could provide a [technological fix](/wiki/Technological_fix "Technological fix") for some impending doom. Reflecting the distrust of government that began in the 1960s in the United States, the brilliant but rebellious scientist became a common theme, often serving a [Cassandra](/wiki/Cassandra "Cassandra")-like role during an impending disaster.

[Biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") (e.g., [cloning](/wiki/Cloning "Cloning")) is a popular scientific element in films as depicted in *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(film) "Jurassic Park (film)")* (cloning of extinct species), *[The Island](/wiki/The_Island_(2005_film) "The Island (2005 film)")* (cloning of humans), and ([genetic modification](/wiki/Genetic_modification "Genetic modification")) in some superhero movies and in the *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(franchise) "Alien (franchise)")* series. [Cybernetics](/wiki/Cybernetics "Cybernetics") and [holographic projections](/wiki/Holography "Holography") as depicted in *[RoboCop](/wiki/RoboCop "RoboCop")* and *[I, Robot](/wiki/I,_Robot_(film) "I, Robot (film)")* are also popularized. [Interstellar travel](/wiki/Interstellar_travel "Interstellar travel") and [teleportation](/wiki/Teleportation "Teleportation") is a popular theme in the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(film_series) "Star Trek (film series)")* series that is achieved through [warp drives](/wiki/Warp_drive "Warp drive") and [transporters](/wiki/Transporter_(Star_Trek) "Transporter (Star Trek)") while [intergalactic travel](/wiki/Intergalactic_travel "Intergalactic travel") is popular in films such as *[Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(film) "Stargate (film)")* and *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* that is achieved through [hyperspace](/wiki/Hyperspace_(science_fiction) "Hyperspace (science fiction)") or [wormholes](/wiki/Wormhole "Wormhole"). [Nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") is also featured in the *Star Trek* series in the form of [replicators](/wiki/Replicator_(Star_Trek) "Replicator (Star Trek)") (utopia), in *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still_(2008_film) "The Day the Earth Stood Still (2008 film)")* in the form of [grey goo](/wiki/Grey_goo "Grey goo") (dystopia), and in *[Iron Man 3](/wiki/Iron_Man_3 "Iron Man 3")* in the form of [extremis (nanotubes)](/wiki/Extremis#extremis "Extremis"). [Force fields](/wiki/Force_shield "Force shield") is a popular theme in *Independence Day* while [invisibility](/wiki/Invisibility "Invisibility") is also popular in *Star Trek*. Arc reactor technology, featured in *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")*, is similar to a [cold fusion](/wiki/Cold_fusion "Cold fusion") device.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Miniaturization technology where people are shrunk to microscopic sizes is featured in films like *[Fantastic Voyage](/wiki/Fantastic_Voyage "Fantastic Voyage")* (1966), *[Honey, I Shrunk the Kids](/wiki/Honey,_I_Shrunk_the_Kids "Honey, I Shrunk the Kids")* (1989), and Marvel's *[Ant-Man](/wiki/Ant-Man_(film) "Ant-Man (film)")* (2015).

The late [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke")'s [third law](/wiki/Clarke%27s_three_laws "Clarke's three laws") states that "any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic". Past science fiction films have depicted "fictional" ("magical") technologies that became present reality. For example, the Personal Access Display Device from *Star Trek* was a precursor of [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"). [Gesture recognition](/wiki/Gesture_recognition "Gesture recognition") in the movie *[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")* is part of current [game consoles](/wiki/Motion_controller "Motion controller"). [Human-level artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence "Artificial general intelligence") is also fast approaching with the advent of [smartphone A.I.](/wiki/Intelligent_personal_assistant "Intelligent personal assistant") while a [working cloaking device / material](/wiki/Cloaking_device "Cloaking device") is the main goal of [stealth technology](/wiki/Stealth_technology "Stealth technology"). [Autonomous cars](/wiki/Autonomous_car "Autonomous car") (e.g. [KITT](/wiki/KITT "KITT") from the *[Knight Rider](/wiki/Knight_Rider_(2008_film) "Knight Rider (2008 film)")* series) and [quantum computers](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer"), like in the movie *[Stealth](/wiki/Stealth_(film) "Stealth (film)")* and *[Transcendence](/wiki/Transcendence_(2014_film) "Transcendence (2014 film)")*, also will be available eventually. Furthermore, although Clarke's laws do not classify ["sufficiently advanced" technologies](/wiki/Disruptive_innovation "Disruptive innovation"), the [Kardashev scale](/wiki/Kardashev_scale "Kardashev scale") measures a civilization's level of technological advancement into types. Due to its exponential nature, sci-fi civilizations usually only attain Type I (harnessing all the energy attainable from a single planet), and strictly speaking often not even that.

### Alien lifeforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Alien lifeforms")]

Main article: [Extraterrestrials in fiction](/wiki/Extraterrestrials_in_fiction "Extraterrestrials in fiction")

The concept of life, particularly intelligent life, having an extraterrestrial origin is a popular staple of science fiction films. Early films often used alien life forms as a threat or peril to the human race, where the invaders were frequently fictional representations of actual military or political threats on Earth as observed in films such as *[Mars Attacks!](/wiki/Mars_Attacks! "Mars Attacks!")*, *[Starship Troopers](/wiki/Starship_Troopers_(film) "Starship Troopers (film)")*, the *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(franchise) "Alien (franchise)")* series, the *[Predator](/wiki/Predator_(franchise) "Predator (franchise)")* series, and *[The Chronicles of Riddick](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Riddick_(franchise) "The Chronicles of Riddick (franchise)")* series. Some aliens were represented as benign and even beneficial in nature in such films as *[Escape to Witch Mountain](/wiki/Escape_to_Witch_Mountain_(1975_film) "Escape to Witch Mountain (1975 film)")*, *[E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial](/wiki/E.T._the_Extra-Terrestrial "E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial")*, *[Close Encounters of the Third Kind](/wiki/Close_Encounters_of_the_Third_Kind "Close Encounters of the Third Kind")*, *[The Fifth Element](/wiki/The_Fifth_Element "The Fifth Element")*, *[The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy](/wiki/The_Hitchhiker%27s_Guide_to_the_Galaxy_(film) "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy (film)")*, *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, *[Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets](/wiki/Valerian_and_the_City_of_a_Thousand_Planets "Valerian and the City of a Thousand Planets")*, and the *[Men in Black](/wiki/Men_in_Black_(film_series) "Men in Black (film series)")* series.

In order to provide subject matter to which audiences can relate, the large majority of intelligent alien races presented in films have an [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") nature, possessing human emotions and motivations. In films like *[Cocoon](/wiki/Cocoon_(film) "Cocoon (film)")*, *[My Stepmother Is an Alien](/wiki/My_Stepmother_Is_an_Alien "My Stepmother Is an Alien")*, *[Species](/wiki/Species_(film) "Species (film)")*, *[Contact](/wiki/Contact_(1997_American_film) "Contact (1997 American film)")*, *[The Box](/wiki/The_Box_(2009_film) "The Box (2009 film)")*, *[Knowing](/wiki/Knowing_(film) "Knowing (film)")*, *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still "The Day the Earth Stood Still")*, and *[The Watch](/wiki/The_Watch_(2012_film) "The Watch (2012 film)")*, the aliens were nearly human in physical appearance, and communicated in a common earth language. However, the aliens in *[Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(film) "Stargate (film)")* and *[Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus_(2012_film) "Prometheus (2012 film)")* were human in physical appearance but communicated in an alien language. A few films have tried to represent intelligent aliens as something utterly different from the usual humanoid shape (e.g. An intelligent life form surrounding an entire planet in *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(1972_film) "Solaris (1972 film)")*, the ball shaped creature in *[Dark Star](/wiki/Dark_Star_(film) "Dark Star (film)")*, microbial-like creatures in *[The Invasion](/wiki/The_Invasion_(film) "The Invasion (film)")*, shape-shifting creatures in *[Evolution](/wiki/Evolution_(2001_film) "Evolution (2001 film)")*). Recent trends in films involve [building-size alien creatures](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju") like in the movie *[Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_(film) "Pacific Rim (film)")* where the [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") has tremendously improved over the previous decades as compared in previous films such as *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1998_film) "Godzilla (1998 film)")*.

### Disaster films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Disaster films")]

Main article: [Disaster film](/wiki/Disaster_film "Disaster film")

A frequent theme among science fiction films is that of impending or actual disaster on an epic scale. These often address a particular concern of the writer by serving as a vehicle of warning against a type of activity, including technological research. In the case of alien invasion films, the creatures can provide as a stand-in for a feared foreign power.

Films that fit into the Disaster film typically also fall into the following general categories:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* **Alien invasion**: Hostile [extraterrestrials](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life_in_popular_culture "Extraterrestrial life in popular culture") arrive and seek to supplant humanity. They are either overwhelmingly powerful or very insidious. Typical examples include *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds_(1953_film) "The War of the Worlds (1953 film)")* (1953), *[Invasion of the Body Snatchers](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Body_Snatchers "Invasion of the Body Snatchers")* (1956), *[Daleks' Invasion Earth 2150 A.D.](/wiki/Daleks%27_Invasion_Earth_2150_A.D. "Daleks' Invasion Earth 2150 A.D.")* (1966), *[Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(1996_film) "Independence Day (1996 film)")* (1996), *[War of the Worlds](/wiki/War_of_the_Worlds_(2005_film) "War of the Worlds (2005 film)")* (2005), *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still_(2008_film) "The Day the Earth Stood Still (2008 film)")* (2008), *[Skyline](/wiki/Skyline_(2010_film) "Skyline (2010 film)")* (2010), *[The Darkest Hour](/wiki/The_Darkest_Hour_(film) "The Darkest Hour (film)")* (2011), *[Battle: Los Angeles](/wiki/Battle:_Los_Angeles "Battle: Los Angeles")* (2011), *[Battleship](/wiki/Battleship_(film) "Battleship (film)")* (2012), *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* (2012), *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(film) "Man of Steel (film)")* (2013), *[Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_(film) "Pacific Rim (film)")* (2013), *[Ender's Game](/wiki/Ender%27s_Game_(film) "Ender's Game (film)")* (2013), *[Pixels](/wiki/Pixels_(2015_film) "Pixels (2015 film)")* (2015), *[Independence Day: Resurgence](/wiki/Independence_Day:_Resurgence "Independence Day: Resurgence")* (2016), and *[Justice League](/wiki/Justice_League_(film) "Justice League (film)")* (2017). *[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")* (1999) takes an alternative look at the subject, involving an extraterrestrial political entity invading planet Naboo for commercial reasons.
* **Environmental disaster**: such as major climate change, or an [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") or [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet") strike. Movies that have employed this theme include *[Soylent Green](/wiki/Soylent_Green "Soylent Green")* (1973), *[Waterworld](/wiki/Waterworld "Waterworld")* (1995), *[Deep Impact](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(film) "Deep Impact (film)")* (1998), *[Armageddon](/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")* (1998), *[The Core](/wiki/The_Core "The Core")* (2003), *[The Day After Tomorrow](/wiki/The_Day_After_Tomorrow "The Day After Tomorrow")* (2004), *[2012](/wiki/2012_(film) "2012 (film)")* (2009), *[Snowpiercer](/wiki/Snowpiercer "Snowpiercer")* (2013) and *[Geostorm](/wiki/Geostorm "Geostorm")* (2017).
* **Man supplanted by technology**: Typically in the form of an all-powerful [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer"), advanced [robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot") or [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg"), or else genetically modified humans or animals. Among the films in this category are the *[Terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(franchise) "Terminator (franchise)")* series, *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)")* trilogy, *[I, Robot](/wiki/I,_Robot_(film) "I, Robot (film)")* (2004), and the *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)")* series.
* **Nuclear war**: Usually in the form of a [dystopic](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia"), post-[holocaust](/wiki/Nuclear_holocaust "Nuclear holocaust") tale of grim survival. Examples of such a storyline can be found in the movies *[Dr. Strangelove](/wiki/Dr._Strangelove "Dr. Strangelove")* (1964), *[Dr. Who and the Daleks](/wiki/Dr._Who_and_the_Daleks "Dr. Who and the Daleks")* (1965), *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(1968_film) "Planet of the Apes (1968 film)")* (1968; [remade in 2001](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(2001_film) "Planet of the Apes (2001 film)")), *[A Boy and His Dog](/wiki/A_Boy_and_His_Dog_(1975_film) "A Boy and His Dog (1975 film)")* (1975), *[Mad Max](/wiki/Mad_Max_(film) "Mad Max (film)")* (1979), *[City of Ember](/wiki/City_of_Ember "City of Ember")* (2008), *[The Book of Eli](/wiki/The_Book_of_Eli "The Book of Eli")* (2010), *[Oblivion](/wiki/Oblivion_(2013_film) "Oblivion (2013 film)")* (2013), *[Mad Max: Fury Road](/wiki/Mad_Max:_Fury_Road "Mad Max: Fury Road")* (2015), and *[Friend of the World](/wiki/Friend_of_the_World "Friend of the World")* (2020).
* **Pandemic**: A highly lethal [disease](/wiki/Disease "Disease"), often one created by man, threatens or wipes out most of humanity in a massive [plague](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic"). This topic has been treated in such films as *[The Andromeda Strain](/wiki/The_Andromeda_Strain_(film) "The Andromeda Strain (film)")* (1971), *[The Omega Man](/wiki/The_Omega_Man "The Omega Man")* (1971), *[12 Monkeys](/wiki/12_Monkeys_(film) "12 Monkeys (film)")* (1995), *[28 Weeks Later](/wiki/28_Weeks_Later "28 Weeks Later")* (2007), *[I Am Legend](/wiki/I_Am_Legend_(film) "I Am Legend (film)")* (2007), and the *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil_(film_series) "Resident Evil (film series)")* series. This version of the genre sometimes mixes with [zombie films](/wiki/Zombie_film "Zombie film") or other [monster movies](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie").

### Monster films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Monster films")]

Main article: [Monster movie](/wiki/Monster_movie "Monster movie")

While monster films do not usually depict danger on a global or epic scale, science fiction film also has a long tradition of movies featuring monster attacks. These differ from similar films in the horror or fantasy genres because science fiction films typically rely on a scientific (or at least pseudo-scientific) rationale for the monster's existence, rather than a supernatural or magical reason. Often, the science fiction film monster is created, awakened, or "evolves" because of the machinations of a mad scientist, a nuclear accident, or a scientific experiment gone awry. Typical examples include *[The Beast from 20,000 Fathoms](/wiki/The_Beast_from_20,000_Fathoms "The Beast from 20,000 Fathoms")* (1953), *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(franchise) "Jurassic Park (franchise)")* films, *[Cloverfield](/wiki/Cloverfield "Cloverfield")*, *[Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_(film) "Pacific Rim (film)")*, *[Split Second (1992 film)](/wiki/Split_Second_(1992_film) "Split Second (1992 film)")*, the *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(franchise) "King Kong (franchise)")* films, and the *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(franchise) "Godzilla (franchise)")* franchise or the many films involving [Frankenstein's monster](/wiki/Frankenstein%27s_monster "Frankenstein's monster").

### Mind and identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mind and identity")]

The core [mental aspects](/wiki/Mind "Mind") of what makes us human has been a staple of science fiction films, particularly since the 1980s. [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott")'s *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* (1982), an adaptation of [Philip K. Dick](/wiki/Philip_K._Dick "Philip K. Dick")'s novel *[Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?](/wiki/Do_Androids_Dream_of_Electric_Sheep%3F "Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?")*, examined what made an organic-creation a human, while the *[RoboCop](/wiki/RoboCop_(franchise) "RoboCop (franchise)")* series saw an [android](/wiki/Android_(robot) "Android (robot)") mechanism fitted with the brain and reprogrammed mind of a human to create a [cyborg](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg"). The idea of brain transfer was not entirely new to science fiction film, as the concept of the "[mad scientist](/wiki/Mad_scientist "Mad scientist")" transferring the human mind to another body is as old as *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* while the idea of corporations behind mind transfer technologies is observed in later films such as *[Gamer](/wiki/Gamer_(2009_film) "Gamer (2009 film)")*, *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*, and *[Surrogates](/wiki/Surrogates_(film) "Surrogates (film)")*.

Films such as *[Total Recall](/wiki/Total_Recall_(1990_film) "Total Recall (1990 film)")* have popularized a thread of films that explore the concept of reprogramming the human mind. The theme of [brainwashing](/wiki/Brainwashing "Brainwashing") in several films of the sixties and seventies including *[A Clockwork Orange](/wiki/A_Clockwork_Orange_(film) "A Clockwork Orange (film)")* and *[The Manchurian Candidate](/wiki/The_Manchurian_Candidate_(1962_film) "The Manchurian Candidate (1962 film)")* coincided with secret real-life government experimentation during [Project MKULTRA](/wiki/Project_MKULTRA "Project MKULTRA"). Voluntary erasure of memory is further explored as themes of the films *[Paycheck](/wiki/Paycheck_(film) "Paycheck (film)")* and *[Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind](/wiki/Eternal_Sunshine_of_the_Spotless_Mind "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind")*. Some films like *[Limitless](/wiki/Limitless_(film) "Limitless (film)")* explore the concept of mind enhancement. The anime series *[Serial Experiments Lain](/wiki/Serial_Experiments_Lain "Serial Experiments Lain")* also explores the idea of reprogrammable reality and memory.

The idea that a human could be entirely represented as a program in a computer was a core element of the film *[Tron](/wiki/Tron "Tron")*. This would be further explored in the film version of *[The Lawnmower Man](/wiki/The_Lawnmower_Man_(film) "The Lawnmower Man (film)")*, *[Transcendence](/wiki/Transcendence_(2014_film) "Transcendence (2014 film)")*, and *[Ready Player One](/wiki/Ready_Player_One_(film) "Ready Player One (film)")* and the idea reversed in *[Virtuosity](/wiki/Virtuosity "Virtuosity")* as computer programs sought to become real persons. In *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)")* series, the [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") world became a real-world prison for humanity, managed by intelligent machines. In movies such as *[eXistenZ](/wiki/EXistenZ "EXistenZ")*, *[The Thirteenth Floor](/wiki/The_Thirteenth_Floor "The Thirteenth Floor")*, and *[Inception](/wiki/Inception "Inception")*, the nature of reality and virtual reality become intermixed with no clear distinguishing boundary.

*[Telekinesis](/wiki/Psychokinesis "Psychokinesis")* and *[telepathy](/wiki/Telepathy "Telepathy")* are featured in movies like *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")*, *[The Last Mimzy](/wiki/The_Last_Mimzy "The Last Mimzy")*, *[Race to Witch Mountain](/wiki/Race_to_Witch_Mountain "Race to Witch Mountain")*, *[Chronicle](/wiki/Chronicle_(film) "Chronicle (film)")*, and *[Lucy](/wiki/Lucy_(2014_film) "Lucy (2014 film)")* while [precognition](/wiki/Precognition "Precognition") is featured in *[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")* as well as in *The Matrix* saga (in which precognition is achieved by knowing the artificial world).

### Robots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Robots")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Transformers_costume_characters_at_Universal_Studios_Hollywood.jpg/250px-Transformers_costume_characters_at_Universal_Studios_Hollywood.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transformers_costume_characters_at_Universal_Studios_Hollywood.jpg)

*[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)")* characters at [Universal Studios Hollywood](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Hollywood "Universal Studios Hollywood")

[Robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot") have been a part of science fiction since the Czech playwright [Karel Čapek](/wiki/Karel_%C4%8Capek "Karel Čapek") coined the word in 1921. In early films, robots were usually played by a human actor in a boxy metal suit, as in *[The Phantom Empire](/wiki/The_Phantom_Empire "The Phantom Empire")*, although the female robot in *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* is an exception. The first depiction of a sophisticated robot in a United States film was [Gort](/wiki/Gort_(The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still) "Gort (The Day the Earth Stood Still)") in *[The Day the Earth Stood Still](/wiki/The_Day_the_Earth_Stood_Still "The Day the Earth Stood Still")*.

Robots in films are often sentient and sometimes sentimental, and they have filled a range of roles in science fiction films. Robots have been supporting characters, such as [Robby the Robot](/wiki/Robby_the_Robot "Robby the Robot") in *[Forbidden Planet](/wiki/Forbidden_Planet "Forbidden Planet")*, Huey, Dewey and Louie in *[Silent Running](/wiki/Silent_Running "Silent Running")*, [Data](/wiki/Data_(Star_Trek) "Data (Star Trek)") in *[Star Trek: The Next Generation](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation "Star Trek: The Next Generation")*, sidekicks (e.g., [C-3PO](/wiki/C-3PO "C-3PO") and [R2-D2](/wiki/R2-D2 "R2-D2") from *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")*, JARVIS from *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")*), and extras, visible in the background to create a futuristic setting (e.g., *[Back to the Future Part II](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future_Part_II "Back to the Future Part II")* (1989), *[Total Recall](/wiki/Total_Recall_(2012_film) "Total Recall (2012 film)")* (2012), *[RoboCop](/wiki/RoboCop_(2014_film) "RoboCop (2014 film)")* (2014)). As well, robots have been formidable movie villains or monsters (e.g., the robot Box in the film *[Logan's Run](/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_(1976_film) "Logan's Run (1976 film)")* (1976), [HAL 9000](/wiki/HAL_9000 "HAL 9000") in *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")*, ARIIA in *[Eagle Eye](/wiki/Eagle_Eye "Eagle Eye")*, robot [Sentinels](/wiki/Sentinel_(comics) "Sentinel (comics)") in *[X-Men: Days of Future Past](/wiki/X-Men:_Days_of_Future_Past "X-Men: Days of Future Past")*, the [battle droids](/wiki/Battle_droid "Battle droid") in the [*Star Wars* prequel trilogy](/wiki/Star_Wars_prequel_trilogy "Star Wars prequel trilogy"), or the huge robot probes seen in *[Monsters vs. Aliens](/wiki/Monsters_vs._Aliens "Monsters vs. Aliens")*). In some cases, robots have even been the leading characters in science fiction films; in the film *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* (1982), many of the characters are [bioengineered](/wiki/Biological_engineering "Biological engineering") [android](/wiki/Android_(robot) "Android (robot)") "[replicants](/wiki/Replicant "Replicant")". This is also present in the animated films *[WALL-E](/wiki/WALL-E "WALL-E")* (2008), *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy_(film) "Astro Boy (film)")* (2009), *[Big Hero 6](/wiki/Big_Hero_6_(film) "Big Hero 6 (film)")* (2014), *[Ghost in the Shell](/wiki/Ghost_in_the_Shell_(2017_film) "Ghost in the Shell (2017 film)")* (2017) and in *[Next Gen](/wiki/Next_Gen_(film) "Next Gen (film)")* (2018).

Films like *[Bicentennial Man](/wiki/Bicentennial_Man_(film) "Bicentennial Man (film)")*, *[A.I. Artificial Intelligence](/wiki/A.I._Artificial_Intelligence "A.I. Artificial Intelligence")*, *[Chappie](/wiki/Chappie_(film) "Chappie (film)")*, and *[Ex Machina](/wiki/Ex_Machina_(film) "Ex Machina (film)")* depicted the emotional fallouts of robots that are self-aware. Other films like *[The Animatrix (The Second Renaissance)](/wiki/The_Second_Renaissance "The Second Renaissance")* present the consequences of mass-producing self-aware androids as humanity succumbs to their robot overlords.

One popular theme in science fiction film is whether robots will someday replace humans, a question raised in the film adaptation of [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov")'s *[I, Robot](/wiki/I,_Robot_(film) "I, Robot (film)")* (in jobs) and in the film *[Real Steel](/wiki/Real_Steel "Real Steel")* (in sports), or whether intelligent robots could develop a conscience and a motivation to protect, take over, or destroy the human race (as depicted in *[The Terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(franchise) "Terminator (franchise)")*, *[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)")*, and in *[Avengers: Age of Ultron](/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron "Avengers: Age of Ultron")*). Another theme is remote [telepresence](/wiki/Telepresence "Telepresence") via [androids](/wiki/Android_(robot) "Android (robot)") as depicted in *[Surrogates](/wiki/Surrogates_(film) "Surrogates (film)")* and *[Iron Man 3](/wiki/Iron_Man_3 "Iron Man 3")*. As artificial intelligence becomes smarter due to [increasing computer power](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law"), some sci-fi dreams have already been realized. For example, the computer [Deep Blue](/wiki/Deep_Blue_(chess_computer) "Deep Blue (chess computer)") beat the world chess champion in 1997 and a documentary film, *[Game Over: Kasparov and the Machine](/wiki/Game_Over:_Kasparov_and_the_Machine "Game Over: Kasparov and the Machine")*, was released in 2003. Another famous computer called [Watson](/wiki/Watson_(computer) "Watson (computer)") defeated the two best human *Jeopardy* (game show) players in 2011 and a NOVA documentary film, *[Smartest Machine on Earth](/wiki/List_of_Nova_episodes#Season_38:_2010–2011 "List of Nova episodes")*, was released in the same year.

[Building-size robots](/wiki/Super_Robot "Super Robot") are also becoming a popular theme in movies as featured in *[Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_(film) "Pacific Rim (film)")*. Future live action films may include an adaptation of popular television series like *[Voltron](/wiki/Voltron "Voltron")* and *[Robotech](/wiki/Robotech "Robotech")*. The [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") robots of *Pacific Rim* and the *[Power Rangers](/wiki/Power_Rangers_(film) "Power Rangers (film)")* (2017) reboot was greatly improved as compared to the original *[Mighty Morphin Power Rangers: The Movie](/wiki/Mighty_Morphin_Power_Rangers:_The_Movie "Mighty Morphin Power Rangers: The Movie")* (1995). While "size does matter", a famous tagline of the movie *Godzilla*, incredibly small robots, called [nanobots](/wiki/Nanorobotics "Nanorobotics"), do matter as well (e.g. Borg [nanoprobes](/wiki/Borg_assimilation "Borg assimilation") in *Star Trek* and nanites in *I, Robot*).

### Time travel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Time travel")]

Main article: [Time travel in fiction](/wiki/Time_travel_in_fiction "Time travel in fiction")

The concept of [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel")—travelling backwards and forwards through time—has always been a popular staple of science fiction film and science fiction television series. Time travel usually involves the use of some type of advanced technology, such as H. G. Wells' classic *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")*, the commercially successful 1980s-era *[Back to the Future](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future_(franchise) "Back to the Future (franchise)")* trilogy, the *[Bill & Ted](/wiki/Bill_%26_Ted "Bill & Ted")* trilogy, the *[Terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(franchise) "Terminator (franchise)")* series, *[Déjà Vu](/wiki/D%C3%A9j%C3%A0_Vu_(2006_film) "Déjà Vu (2006 film)")* (2006), *[Source Code](/wiki/Source_Code_(film) "Source Code (film)")* (2011), *[Edge of Tomorrow](/wiki/Edge_of_Tomorrow_(film) "Edge of Tomorrow (film)")* (2014), and *[Predestination](/wiki/Predestination_(film) "Predestination (film)")* (2014). Other movies, such as the *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(1968_film) "Planet of the Apes (1968 film)")* series, *[Timeline](/wiki/Timeline_(2003_film) "Timeline (2003 film)")* (2003) and *[The Last Mimzy](/wiki/The_Last_Mimzy "The Last Mimzy")* (2007), explained their depictions of time travel by drawing on physics concepts such as the [special relativity](/wiki/Special_relativity "Special relativity") phenomenon of time dilation (which could occur if a spaceship was travelling near the speed of light) and [wormholes](/wiki/Wormhole "Wormhole"). Some films show time travel not being attained from advanced technology, but rather from an inner source or personal power, such as the 2000s-era films *[Donnie Darko](/wiki/Donnie_Darko "Donnie Darko")*, *[Mr. Nobody](/wiki/Mr._Nobody_(film) "Mr. Nobody (film)")*, *[The Butterfly Effect](/wiki/The_Butterfly_Effect_(film) "The Butterfly Effect (film)")*, and *[X-Men: Days of Future Past](/wiki/X-Men:_Days_of_Future_Past "X-Men: Days of Future Past")*.

More conventional time travel movies use technology to bring the past to life in the present, or in a present that lies in our future. The film *[Iceman](/wiki/Iceman_(1984_film) "Iceman (1984 film)")* (1984) told the story of the reanimation of a frozen [Neanderthal](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal"). The film *[Freejack](/wiki/Freejack "Freejack")* (1992) shows time travel used to pull victims of horrible deaths forward in time a split-second before their demise, and then use their bodies for spare parts.

A common theme in time travel film is the paradoxical nature of travelling through time. In the [French New Wave](/wiki/French_New_Wave "French New Wave") film *[La jetée](/wiki/La_jet%C3%A9e "La jetée")* (1962), director [Chris Marker](/wiki/Chris_Marker "Chris Marker") depicts the self-fulfilling aspect of a person being able to see their future by showing a child who witnesses the death of his future self. *La Jetée* was the inspiration for *[12 Monkeys](/wiki/12_Monkeys_(film) "12 Monkeys (film)")*, (1995) director [Terry Gilliam](/wiki/Terry_Gilliam "Terry Gilliam")'s film about time travel, memory and madness.
