The *[Back to the Future](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future_(franchise) "Back to the Future (franchise)")* trilogy and *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine_(2002_film) "The Time Machine (2002 film)")* go one step further and explore the result of altering the past, while in *[Star Trek: First Contact](/wiki/Star_Trek:_First_Contact "Star Trek: First Contact")* (1996) and *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")* (2009) the crew must rescue the Earth from having its past altered by time-travelling [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg") and alien races.

## Genre as commentary on social issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Genre as commentary on social issues")]

The science fiction film genre has long served as useful means of discussing sensitive topical issues without arousing controversy, and it often provides thoughtful social commentary on potential unforeseen future issues. The fictional setting allows for a deeper examination and reflection of the ideas presented, with the perspective of a viewer watching remote events. Most controversial issues in science fiction films tend to fall into two general storylines, [Utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") or [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia"). Either a society will become better or worse in the future. Because of controversy, most science fiction films will fall into the [dystopian film](/wiki/List_of_dystopian_films "List of dystopian films") category rather than the Utopian category.

The types of commentary and controversy presented in science fiction films often illustrate the particular concerns of the periods in which they were produced. Early science fiction films expressed fears about automation replacing workers and the dehumanization of society through science and technology. For example, *[The Man in the White Suit](/wiki/The_Man_in_the_White_Suit "The Man in the White Suit")* (1951) used a science fiction concept as a means to satirize postwar British "establishment" conservatism, industrial capitalists, and trade unions. Another example is *[HAL 9000](/wiki/HAL_9000 "HAL 9000")* from *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (1968). He controls the shuttle, and later harms its crew. "Kubrick's vision reveals technology as a competitive force that must be defeated in order for humans to evolve."[[14]](#cite_note-14) Later films explored the fears of environmental catastrophe, technology-created disasters, or overpopulation, and how they would impact society and individuals (e.g. *[Soylent Green](/wiki/Soylent_Green "Soylent Green")*, *[Elysium](/wiki/Elysium_(film) "Elysium (film)")*).

The monster movies of the 1950s—like *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1954_film) "Godzilla (1954 film)")* (1954)—served as stand-ins for fears of [nuclear war](/wiki/Nuclear_warfare "Nuclear warfare"), [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and views on the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 1970s, science fiction films also became an effective way of satirizing contemporary social mores with *[Silent Running](/wiki/Silent_Running "Silent Running")* and *[Dark Star](/wiki/Dark_Star_(film) "Dark Star (film)")* presenting hippies in space as a riposte to the militaristic types that had dominated earlier films.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s *[A Clockwork Orange](/wiki/A_Clockwork_Orange_(film) "A Clockwork Orange (film)")* presented a horrific vision of youth culture, portraying a youth gang engaged in [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape") and [murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder"), along with disturbing scenes of forced [psychological conditioning](/wiki/Classical_conditioning "Classical conditioning") serving to comment on societal responses to [crime](/wiki/Crime "Crime").

*[Logan's Run](/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_(1976_film) "Logan's Run (1976 film)")* depicted a futuristic [swingers](/wiki/Swinging_(sexual_practice) "Swinging (sexual practice)")' utopia that practiced euthanasia as a form of population control and *[The Stepford Wives](/wiki/The_Stepford_Wives_(1975_film) "The Stepford Wives (1975 film)")* anticipated a reaction to the [women's liberation movement](/wiki/Women%27s_liberation_movement "Women's liberation movement"). *[Enemy Mine](/wiki/Enemy_Mine_(film) "Enemy Mine (film)")* demonstrated that the foes we have come to hate are often just like us, even if they appear alien.

Contemporary science fiction films continue to explore social and political issues. One recent example is *[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")* (2002), debuting in the months after the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001, and focused on the issues of police powers, privacy and civil liberties in a near-future United States. Some movies like *[The Island](/wiki/The_Island_(2005_film) "The Island (2005 film)")* (2005) and *[Never Let Me Go](/wiki/Never_Let_Me_Go_(2010_film) "Never Let Me Go (2010 film)")* (2010) explore the issues surrounding cloning.

More recently, the headlines surrounding events such as the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), [international terrorism](/wiki/International_terrorism "International terrorism"), the [avian influenza](/wiki/Avian_influenza "Avian influenza") scare, and United States [anti-immigration laws](/wiki/Opposition_to_immigration "Opposition to immigration") have found their way into the consciousness of contemporary filmmakers. The film *[V for Vendetta](/wiki/V_for_Vendetta_(film) "V for Vendetta (film)")* (2006) drew inspiration from controversial issues such as the [Patriot Act](/wiki/Patriot_Act "Patriot Act") and the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] while science fiction thrillers such as *[Children of Men](/wiki/Children_of_Men "Children of Men")* (also 2006), *[District 9](/wiki/District_9 "District 9")* (2009), and *[Elysium](/wiki/Elysium_(film) "Elysium (film)")* (2013) commented on diverse social issues such as [xenophobia](/wiki/Xenophobia "Xenophobia"), [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda"), and [cognitive dissonance](/wiki/Cognitive_dissonance "Cognitive dissonance"). *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")* (2009) had remarkable resemblance to colonialism of native land, mining by multinational-corporations and the Iraq War.

### Future noir

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Future noir")]

[Lancaster University](/wiki/Lancaster_University "Lancaster University") professor Jamaluddin Bin Aziz argues that as science fiction has evolved and expanded, it has fused with other film genres such as [gothic](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction") [thrillers](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") and [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir"). When science fiction integrates film noir elements, Bin Aziz calls the resulting hybrid form "future noir", a form which "... encapsulates a [postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern "Postmodern") encounter with generic persistence, creating a mixture of irony, pessimism, prediction, extrapolation, bleakness and nostalgia." Future noir films such as *[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_(1985_film) "Brazil (1985 film)")*, *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")*, *[12 Monkeys](/wiki/12_Monkeys_(film) "12 Monkeys (film)")*, *[Dark City](/wiki/Dark_City_(1998_film) "Dark City (1998 film)")*, and *[Children of Men](/wiki/Children_of_Men "Children of Men")* use a [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist") who is "...increasingly dubious, alienated and fragmented", at once "dark and playful like the characters in Gibson's *[Neuromancer](/wiki/Neuromancer "Neuromancer")*, yet still with the "... shadow of [Philip Marlowe](/wiki/Philip_Marlowe "Philip Marlowe")..."

Future noir films that are set in a [post-apocalyptic](/wiki/Post-apocalyptic "Post-apocalyptic") world "...restructure and re-represent society in a parody of the atmospheric world usually found in noir's construction of a city—dark, bleak and beguiled." Future noir films often intermingle elements of the gothic thriller genre, such as *[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")*, which makes references to [occult](/wiki/Occult "Occult") practices, and *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")*, with its tagline "In space, no one can hear you scream", and a space vessel, Nostromo, "that hark[s] back to images of the haunted house in the gothic horror tradition". Bin Aziz states that films such as [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron")’s *[The Terminator](/wiki/The_Terminator "The Terminator")* are a subgenre of "techno noir" that create "...an atmospheric feast of noir darkness and a double-edged world that is not what it seems."[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Film versus literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Film versus literature")]

When compared to science-fiction [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), science-fiction films often rely less on the human imagination and more upon action scenes and [special effect](/wiki/Special_effects "Special effects")-created alien creatures and exotic backgrounds. Since the 1970s, film audiences have come to expect a high standard for special effects in science-fiction films.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In some cases, science fiction-themed films superimpose an exotic, futuristic setting onto what would not otherwise be a science-fiction tale. Nevertheless, some critically acclaimed science-fiction movies have followed in the path of science-fiction literature, using story development to explore abstract concepts.

### Influence of science fiction authors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction_film&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Influence of science fiction authors")]

[Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne") (1828–1905) became the first major science-fiction author whose works film-makers adapted for the screen - with [Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès")' *[Le Voyage dans la Lune](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")* (1902) and *[20,000 lieues sous les mers](/wiki/Under_the_Seas "Under the Seas")* (1907), which used Verne's scenarios as a framework for fantastic visuals. By the time Verne's work fell out of copyright in 1950, the adaptations were generally adapted as [costume dramas](/wiki/Costume_drama "Costume drama") with a [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") aesthetic. Verne's works have been adapted a number of times since then, including *[20,000 Leagues Under the Sea](/wiki/20,000_Leagues_Under_the_Sea_(1954_film) "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea (1954 film)")* (1954), *[From the Earth to the Moon](/wiki/From_the_Earth_to_the_Moon_(film) "From the Earth to the Moon (film)")* (1958), and two film versions of *[Journey to the Center of the Earth](/wiki/Journey_to_the_Center_of_the_Earth "Journey to the Center of the Earth")* in 1959 and 2008.

[H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells")'s novels *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man_(1933_film) "The Invisible Man (1933 film)")*, *[Things to Come](/wiki/Things_to_Come "Things to Come")* and *[The Island of Doctor Moreau](/wiki/The_Island_of_Doctor_Moreau "The Island of Doctor Moreau")* were all adapted into films during his lifetime (1866–1946), while [*The War of the Worlds*](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds_(novel) "The War of the Worlds (novel)"), updated in 1953 and again in 2005, was adapted to film at least four times altogether. *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")* has had two film versions (1960 and 2002) while *[Sleeper](/wiki/Sleeper_(1973_film) "Sleeper (1973 film)")* in part is a pastiche of Wells's 1910 novel *[The Sleeper Awakes](/wiki/The_Sleeper_Awakes "The Sleeper Awakes")*.

With the drop-off in interest in science-fiction films during the 1940s, few of the "golden age" science-fiction authors made it to the screen. A novella by [John W. Campbell](/wiki/John_W._Campbell "John W. Campbell") provided the basis for *[The Thing from Another World](/wiki/The_Thing_from_Another_World "The Thing from Another World")* (1951). [Robert A. Heinlein](/wiki/Robert_A._Heinlein "Robert A. Heinlein") contributed to the screenplay for *[Destination Moon](/wiki/Destination_Moon_(film) "Destination Moon (film)")* (1950), but none of his major works were adapted for the screen until the 1990s: *[The Puppet Masters](/wiki/The_Puppet_Masters_(film) "The Puppet Masters (film)")* (1994) and *[Starship Troopers](/wiki/Starship_Troopers_(film) "Starship Troopers (film)")* (1997). The fiction of [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") (1920–1992) influenced the *Star Wars* and *Star Trek* films, but it was not until 1988 that a film version of one of his short stories ([*Nightfall*](/wiki/Nightfall_(Asimov) "Nightfall (Asimov)")) was produced. The first major motion-picture adaptation of a full-length Asimov work was [*Bicentennial Man*](/wiki/Bicentennial_Man_(film) "Bicentennial Man (film)") (1999) (based on the short stories *Bicentennial Man* (1976) and *The Positronic Man* (1992), the latter co-written with Robert Silverberg), although [*I, Robot*](/wiki/I,_Robot_(film) "I, Robot (film)") (2004), a film loosely based on [Asimov's book of short stories](/wiki/I,_Robot "I, Robot") by the same name, drew more attention.

The 1968 film adaptation of some of the stories of science-fiction author [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke") as [*2001: A Space Odyssey*](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)") won the [Academy Award for Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Visual Effects") and offered thematic complexity not typically associated with the science-fiction genre at the time. Its sequel, *[2010: The Year We Make Contact](/wiki/2010:_The_Year_We_Make_Contact "2010: The Year We Make Contact")* (inspired to Clarke's *[2010: Odyssey Two](/wiki/2010:_Odyssey_Two "2010: Odyssey Two")*), was commercially successful but less highly regarded by critics. Reflecting the times, two earlier science-fiction works by [Ray Bradbury](/wiki/Ray_Bradbury "Ray Bradbury") were adapted for cinema in the 1960s: [*Fahrenheit 451*](/wiki/Fahrenheit_451_(1966_film) "Fahrenheit 451 (1966 film)") (1966) and *[The Illustrated Man](/wiki/The_Illustrated_Man_(film) "The Illustrated Man (film)")* (1969). [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut")'s *[Slaughterhouse-Five](/wiki/Slaughterhouse-Five_(film) "Slaughterhouse-Five (film)")* was filmed in 1971 and *[Breakfast of Champions](/wiki/Breakfast_of_Champions "Breakfast of Champions")* in 1998.

[Philip K. Dick](/wiki/Philip_K._Dick "Philip K. Dick")'s fiction has been used in a number of science-fiction films, in part because it evokes the paranoia[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] that has been a central feature of the genre. Films based on Dick's works include *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* (1982), [*Total Recall*](/wiki/Total_Recall_(1990_film) "Total Recall (1990 film)") (1990), [*Impostor*](/wiki/Impostor_(2002_film) "Impostor (2002 film)") (2001), [*Minority Report*](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)") (2002), [*Paycheck*](/wiki/Paycheck_(film) "Paycheck (film)") (2003), [*A Scanner Darkly*](/wiki/A_Scanner_Darkly_(film) "A Scanner Darkly (film)") (2006), and *[The Adjustment Bureau](/wiki/The_Adjustment_Bureau "The Adjustment Bureau")* (2011). These films represent loose adaptations of the original stories, with the exception of *A Scanner Darkly*, which is more inclined to Dick's novel.
